name: Recruitment Channel Optimization
consequences: Pulling this lever accelerates participant enrollment, but may increase screening workload and risk of enrolling ineligible subjects. It also raises the cost per participant if additional triage resources are required.
options: Implement a hybrid referral pipeline that relies on hospital EMR alerts, patient portal outreach, and community outreach events.
Implement a referral pipeline that uses targeted physician outreach, patient self-referral portals, and regional sleep clinic collaborations.
Implement a referral pipeline that incorporates automated triage algorithms, nurse-led screening calls, and partnership with national sleep disorder registries.
review: Core tension: balancing rapid enrollment against screening accuracy; options overlook the need for real-time eligibility verification during triage, risking over-enrollment of ineligible participants.

name: Annotation Workflow Efficiency
consequences: Pulling this lever reduces manual scoring time, but may compromise inter‑rater reliability if shortcuts are adopted. It also increases reliance on technician training and may extend shift durations.
options: Implement a tiered annotation system that assigns preliminary scoring to trained technicians, followed by double-checks by senior reviewers.
Implement a collaborative annotation platform that synchronizes real-time scoring across multiple reviewers, enabling instant consensus and error flagging.
Implement a semi-automated annotation workflow that uses rule-based event detection to pre-label segments, then requires human confirmation before final scoring.
review: Core tension: speeding annotation while maintaining quality; options ignore the impact of technician fatigue on scoring consistency over long shifts.

name: Equipment Acquisition Strategy
consequences: Pulling this lever influences data fidelity and budget; selecting high-end sensors may improve event capture but increase maintenance complexity and long-term costs.
options: Implement a phased procurement plan that prioritizes essential dry-electrode headbands and mattress sensors, adding full PSG equipment only after pilot validation.
Implement a modular equipment architecture that allows interchangeable sensor modules, enabling rapid upgrades and parallel testing of new acquisition technologies.
Implement a cost-sharing partnership with a medical device manufacturer to secure discounted equipment in exchange for anonymized data access for future research.
review: Core tension: balancing data quality with budget constraints; options overlook the long-term maintenance costs and potential obsolescence of high-end PSG systems.

name: Participant Safety and Ethics Protocol
consequences: Pulling this lever enhances participant protection, but may increase administrative burden and delay enrollment; strict safety protocols could limit flexibility in event response.
options: Implement a continuous safety monitoring framework that integrates real-time vitals, automated alarm thresholds, and rapid-response staffing protocols.
Implement a participant education program that delivers pre-admission training videos, in-room safety checklists, and post-discharge follow-up calls.
Implement a risk stratification algorithm that flags high-risk participants for additional overnight monitoring and tailored safety measures.
review: Core tension: ensuring safety without stifling recruitment; options fail to address how safety measures might deter participants with mild symptoms.

name: Data Sharing and Privacy Governance
consequences: Pulling this lever increases transparency and scientific impact, but may expose sensitive data and require robust governance; public sharing is limited to de-identified data.
options: Implement a tiered data access model that grants researchers controlled, de-identified datasets while preserving participant anonymity through strict encryption.
Implement a data use agreement framework that requires external researchers to sign confidentiality clauses and submit detailed data handling plans.
Implement a community advisory board that reviews data sharing requests and ensures alignment with local ethical standards and participant expectations.
review: Core tension: balancing openness with privacy; options ignore the potential for re-identification through cross-referencing ancillary data.

name: Event-Triage Algorithm Development
consequences: Pulling this lever reduces manual review load, but algorithm accuracy must be validated against human scorers; risk of false positives may increase workload.
options: Implement a supervised learning pipeline that trains classifiers on manually scored events, then applies threshold tuning to balance sensitivity and specificity.
Implement a hybrid rule-based system that flags events based on physiological signatures, followed by human confirmation to refine model parameters.
Implement an iterative feedback loop that incorporates scorer adjudication outcomes into continuous model retraining, ensuring performance improves over time.
review: Core tension: reducing workload while maintaining accuracy; options overlook the need for ongoing human oversight to prevent drift in algorithm performance.

name: Sleep Suite Utilization Optimization
consequences: Pulling this lever increases occupancy of suites, reducing idle nights and maximizing data yield, but may raise staff workload and equipment wear. Higher utilization can also compress participant comfort if suites are overbooked, potentially affecting adherence.
options: Deploy a predictive scheduling system that allocates participants to suites based on anticipated event frequency, maximizing nightly data yield while preserving buffer capacity.
Introduce a rotating maintenance protocol that performs overnight cleaning and calibration on each suite, extending equipment lifespan without interrupting participant stays.
Offer participants a choice between shared and private suites with tiered compensation, encouraging efficient space use while respecting individual comfort preferences.
review: Balancing suite occupancy against staff fatigue and equipment wear; the options overlook the need for real‑time workload monitoring to prevent overtime.

name: Participant Retention and Engagement
consequences: Enhancing retention improves adherence and event capture, but raises per‑participant costs and demands additional support staff. Over‑engagement may also pressure participants, impacting data quality.
options: Deploy a personalized engagement protocol that schedules regular check‑ins and delivers tailored sleep‑hygiene resources, fostering participant commitment throughout the stay.
Introduce a gamified progress dashboard that displays nightly sleep metrics and offers small incentives for consistent participation, motivating sustained engagement.
Provide flexible meal and activity options that align with participants’ cultural preferences, reducing perceived burden and encouraging longer stays.
review: Enhancing retention may raise per‑participant costs and staff demands; the options ignore the need for scalable support infrastructure.

name: Longitudinal Data Integration
consequences: Integrating multi‑modal streams across nights enriches phenotyping, yet increases computational load and storage needs. Complex harmonization may also delay analysis.
options: Deploy a modular data ingestion framework that automatically tags and aligns multi‑modal sensor streams in real time, facilitating seamless longitudinal aggregation.
Implement a cloud‑based analytics layer that aggregates nightly datasets, enabling cross‑night comparisons without imposing local storage bottlenecks.
Introduce a standardized metadata schema that captures contextual variables such as medication changes and sleep environment, supporting longitudinal trend analysis.
review: Integrating longitudinal data enhances phenotyping but may overwhelm storage and processing; the options overlook the need for incremental data compression strategies.

name: Clinical Workflow Synchronization
consequences: Aligning technician shifts with event patterns improves response times, but reduces flexibility for unexpected events and adds coordination overhead.
options: Deploy a real‑time event alert system that notifies technicians via wearable devices, ensuring immediate response to flagged events.
Implement a shift‑rotation schedule that aligns technician coverage with peak event windows identified from historical data, optimizing response efficiency.
Introduce a cross‑disciplinary briefing protocol that synchronizes sleep physicians, technicians, and data engineers before each night, reducing miscommunication.
review: Synchronizing workflows boosts response speed but may constrain flexibility for emergent events; the options miss contingency planning for technician absences.

name: Regulatory Compliance Alignment
consequences: Ensuring GDPR and trial compliance reduces legal risk, but can slow study initiation and increase administrative burden. It may also limit data sharing scope.
options: Create a dedicated compliance officer position that monitors evolving GDPR and clinical trial guidelines, ensuring timely protocol adjustments.
Implement a centralized audit trail platform that logs every data access and consent update, enabling rapid regulatory inspections.
Introduce a participant education module that explains data usage and privacy rights, fostering informed consent and reducing future disputes.
review: Aligning with regulations protects the study but may delay recruitment; the options overlook the need for a phased consent strategy.

name: Funding Sustainability Strategy
consequences: Securing diverse funding supports expansion and advanced analytics, yet requires additional reporting and stakeholder engagement. It may shift priorities toward funding metrics.
options: Build a tiered grant portfolio that targets national, European, and industry funding streams, diversifying revenue sources.
Implement a transparent financial dashboard that tracks budget utilization against milestones, offering stakeholders real‑time insight into resource allocation.
Introduce a stakeholder advisory panel that convenes quarterly to review progress and align funding priorities with scientific objectives.
review: Securing diverse funding supports growth but may impose reporting burdens; the options ignore the need for automated compliance reporting.

name: Participant Sleep Environment Calibration
consequences: Adjusting room temperature, lighting, and acoustic conditions to mirror natural circadian cues increases the likelihood of capturing authentic parasomnia events. This may raise equipment and maintenance costs and require staff training to manage dynamic settings.
options: Deploy a dynamic environmental control system that adjusts room temperature and lighting to mirror natural circadian cues.
Implement a standardized pre‑sleep routine protocol that participants follow each night to reduce variability in baseline arousal.
Introduce a nightly acoustic masking protocol that uses low‑frequency soundscapes to dampen external noise.
review: Core tension between environmental realism and operational complexity; options overlook the need to validate that adjustments do not interfere with sensor signal integrity.

name: Real‑Time Event Escalation Protocol
consequences: A structured escalation workflow for flagged events enables rapid deployment of full PSG, improving data resolution for rare episodes. It increases technician workload and may delay monitoring of other nights.
options: Establish a tiered alert system that triggers full PSG deployment within 30 minutes of technician flagging a suspected event.
Create a shared digital log that allows technicians to annotate and prioritize events in real time, enabling coordinated escalation.
Introduce a rotating on‑call specialist who reviews flagged events during the night and decides on PSG escalation.
review: Tension between rapid data capture and staffing constraints; options miss the impact of on‑call specialist fatigue on decision quality.

name: Cross‑Disciplinary Data Integration
consequences: Consolidating physiological, behavioral, and environmental streams into a unified platform enriches phenotyping but adds computational and governance complexity.
options: Build a centralized data warehouse that consolidates EEG, video, audio, and sensor data for joint analysis.
Deploy a modular analytics pipeline that processes each data modality separately before merging results for cross‑validation.
Implement a real‑time fusion engine that overlays multimodal signals to flag potential events automatically.
review: Core tension between data richness and privacy safeguards; options ignore the need for robust de‑identification before cross‑modality merging.

name: Ethical Oversight of Video Recording
consequences: Strengthening safeguards around video capture improves participant trust and recruitment willingness, but introduces stricter consent procedures and potential enrollment delays.
options: Introduce a participant‑controlled video pause button that can be activated during any night.
Implement a third‑party video audit system that reviews footage for privacy compliance before storage.
Offer participants the option to have video recordings deleted after a set period, with data retained only in anonymized form.
review: Tension between data utility and privacy; options fail to address how deletion requests affect longitudinal data continuity.

name: Longitudinal Participant Follow‑Up Strategy
consequences: Systematic post‑discharge monitoring extends the data set and deepens phenotyping, yet increases participant burden and risks attrition.
options: Schedule monthly home‑based actigraphy sessions for participants after discharge to monitor ongoing episodes.
Provide participants with a wearable sleep tracker that syncs data to the central repository for continuous monitoring.
Conduct quarterly telehealth check‑ins that include structured sleep diaries and symptom questionnaires.
review: Tension between longitudinal depth and participant compliance; options overlook the challenge of ensuring data quality from home‑based devices.

name: Resource Allocation for Enhanced PSG Nights
consequences: Optimizing budget and staffing for PSG nights improves event resolution but may create bottlenecks in technician availability and increase costs.
options: Allocate a dedicated PSG technician for each enhanced night, ensuring full coverage and rapid setup.
Use a shared technician pool that rotates between enhanced and standard nights, balancing workload.
Implement a remote PSG setup protocol that allows technicians to configure equipment from a control room, reducing on‑site presence.
review: Core tension between data fidelity and cost efficiency; options miss the impact of remote setup on signal quality and participant comfort.
